channel.title: Tabfugnic
channel.description: My life as a Software Developer

item.title: Hello world!
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded: Welcome to Wordpress.com. This is your first post. Edit or delete it and start blogging!

wp:comment.wp:comment_content:
Hi, this is a comment.
To delete a comment, just log in, and view the posts' comments, there you will have the option to edit or delete them.

item.title: The wild world of blogging
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic

item.title: Oily
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
Just from the news perspective, these past two weeks have been crazy. That massive oil spill that seems to threaten all of the gulf coast. It seems increasingly imperative to keep this off of the shore. The entire thing has the potential to destroy the fishing market in the gulf coast, if it hasn't all ready. One amazing thing I heard and I didn't realize this, but apparently the Exxon spill off of Alaska has left a lasting impact on the shore where its been over 20 years and still oil can be found all over the shore and fish in the area are still being caught with oil in their bellies. That's terrifying. I've always been a strong proponent of an oil free economy, at least in the realm of consumer automobiles among other things. I've never had the funds to support such a thing, but I have actively pursued gas and oil free alternatives when I have had the chance. I rarely if ever go on campus with my car and when I can I ride the train. I personally wish that the price of oil would skyrocket making it economically infeasible to use as a fuel source and thus in this free market society the market would work to create an alternative. Right now, there is little incentive. Many would like to see more alternatives on the market, but the cost involved ultimately finds that it is cheaper just to go with an old car that consumes slightly more gas. Some of course don't believe in global warming and thus don't care if petroleum based products are used. Even if there is doubt to the possibility of global warming the thought of maintaining oil as central to our way of life is fundamentally flawed in too many ways to count.
First, the most obvious thing we've seen from this recent spill is that oil is dirty. Quite frankly it's dirty as hell. It's hard to clean up and has profound affects on the wildlife around that would come in contact with it. Even if we could take every possible precaution with flawless precision, accidents still happen. When they do, the outcome is disastrous. The oil will eventual settle and the wildlife and environment will go back to normal, but the time that it takes will be well beyond our lifetime.
Second, it's a limited resource. There are debates as to how soon we will run out, but what is abundantly clear is that a fossil fuel is very finite and if we don't run out in the next ten years or fifty years we will run out within a foreseeable future.
Lastly, it's costly. But this works into the how it is dirty and nonrenewable. Oil has to be found transported, refined, stored, transported and ultimately it can get to your car. I don't actually know if the process is more complicated than that, but it is at least that complicated. Unfortunately, there aren't really any viable power supplies that can create a similar amount of power without having the same steps. Though there is the possibility of getting an electric car that runs off a grid that uses only wind power, that'd be sweet. Of course there are mountain of problem associated with using a solely electric based car, mainly that goes into poor power storage technology. That's a totally different issue.
I cannot fathom why everyone is so bent on maintaining this filthy, expensive and limited resource. My feeling is that so long as it is cheap and relatively easy to get we will never get off of our dependency of oil. The only hope we have is to have technology that effectively makes it the more costly option, even in the short term. I hope that day comes soon.
@Tab

item.title: Graduation
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded: Tomorrow I graduate. Well, not for real. I'll walk at my graduation ceremony, but I won't technically know if I've graduated college until about a month from now. I can't say that I'm incredibly excited about the whole thing. Sure, I'll be doing without homework and right after I'm done I'll be heading off to the wild world of east asia, but I'm mostly indifferent. I think most of this has to do with how much work is still to be done in the next three days and then how much traveling will need to be done after that. Well I finally get to Taiwan I'll be happy, until I realize that I'm completely lost in every respect then it might take a bit more to get used to. Until then I need to look forward to my graduation, despite all of the work that needs to be done until then and in conjunction.

item.title: Using Ubuntu 10.04 Lucid Lynx Netbook Edition on Acer 532h
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
There are several listings all over the Internet on all the problems associated with the 532h and the Ubuntu Netbook Edition, but I'd like to consolidate some of the problems.  This guide will hopefully add more redundancy to the searches made by others and point them in the right direction. All solutions I've come across are from here https://wiki.ubuntu.com/HardwareSupport/Machines/Netbooks Known problems:
  1. Trackpad multigesture support
  2. Trackpad side scrolling support
  3. SD Card Reader
  4. Microphone
  5. Some Fn + Key combinations
  6. Brightness controls are a bit funky
Some Solutions
  • Trackpad side scroller fix
    Go to terminal and type the command:
    gksu gedit /etc/modprobe.d/psmouse.conf
    When that comes up add the line:
    options psmouse proto=imps
    Reboot and it should work
    • Microphone workaround
    To fix the microphone is a bit more difficult, there is no permanent fix that I'm aware of You just need to in the terminal type the following:
    /bin/sh/ -c "PULSE-SERVER=127.0.0.1 program-name"
    Where program name is the command line name of skype, gnome-sound-recorder, etc.

item.title: Going to Taiwan
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
Well, I thought I had written this out in a word document on the train, but apparently it's gone now. So I'll just need to retype it.
To anyone who is thinking of staying in Taiwan for longer than 30 days.
I had planned to take a 98 day trip to Taiwan and spend it there without a visa, as the process to get the visa seemed somewhat daunting. I had also been excited about the idea of traveling to other places in Southeast Asia. I decided early on that maybe it'd just be easier to just go in on a landing visa and jump out every thirty days and go somewhere new. Sounds stupid now, but at the time I thought it was a great idea. It would give me the chance to see the area, more of the world and would force me to do it. Then I came to realize that cost is a pretty big issue for a recently graduated college student and with my current supply of funds running dry and my new source smaller than the last I decided this wasn't such a good idea in relation to the overall cost. It's also somewhat shady.
Being here in New York before I leave for Taipei, I used this opportunity to put what I needed together and apply for a 60 day multiple entry visa. I know what you might be thinking, why only 60 days, aren't you staying for 98 days? True, but the good thing about this, is according to the Taipei Economic and Cultural Office, the visa can be extended twice another 60 days each time, for a total of 180 days. Well beyond what I plan to do, but a much safer bet. The good news about this whole thing as opposed to paying $140 for each time I come back into Taiwan and forcing myself to pay airfare each time out. I can now just go out of the country when I choose and don't have to make as much of a financial commitment.
I'm excited for Taiwan, but like every place it seems I go for the past five years, money is tight and making sure that I'm spending the least I can while still having the fullest experience.
@Tab

item.title: My flight to Hong Kong
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
I had a post for this ready that I was typing up on the plane, but I consumed four glasses of wine before writing. It was not only largely incoherent, but also very boring. So instead I'm going to write a new one that will be equally as boring, but I will be completely sober.
That flight was long. So so long. Sixteen hours worth of just sitting in the middle seat of the middle section of a plane. I'm a pretty big guy and thankfully these planes have more than the average domestic flight, but it was still cramped. I did manage to get up a few times and stretch. Worst of all, I had a preteen girl on the left of me in an aisle seat that clearly didn't need much leg room and kept on falling asleep on me. The one time I tried to pass the old woman on the right of me, she reminded me that the little girl was smaller and therefore easier to get by and I should just take that route each time I needed to get up.
Like any international flight, nothing really exciting happened. I watched a bunch of movies and I was far too uncomfortable to even try to read something. I didn't sleep the entire time, though I definitely tried and right now I've been up for somewhere in the ball park of 35 hours. Though I might be exaggerating. Either way I'm tired. Still a bit nervous too. This will be, as I've said before, the first time I've ever gone to a country by myself and on top of that I'm going to be staying with people I've never met that speak little to no English. It should definitely teach me how to understand and speak mandarin a whooole lot more.
Until then, I anxiously await my flight.
@Tab

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Thanks for posting! I'm sorry your flight was crummy. Hopefully things will start to go uphill from here.

item.title: One Week
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
It's now been a week since I arrived and I've all ready done so much.  Just friday I watched a man kill two rattle snakes and two taiwanese poisonous somethings.  The rattlesnake is all ready pretty well known, but the Taiwanese snake, according to the men there, can kill a person in half an hour.   Both of these snakes are considered rare and are protected either locally or globally.  A few moments later I drank the rice wine blood mixture that had a bit of an organ that when described to me sounded like the spleen.


Though this to me was a high point the rest of the week was full of adventure as well. I tried for the first time something called a Betelnut. A seed that is wrapped in a leaf and chewed like tobacco.  When it came time to spit the fluids out they came out as a blood red color they made it look like I had internal bleeding.  After chewing for only a few minutes I felt almost drunk.


The dragonboat festival happened on Wednesday and though it's labeled as the second most celebrated event in Taiwan and China it cannot hold a candle to Chinese New Year.  People come to race in boats shaped like, you guessed it, a dragon.  The festival we went to was held in a harbor, people from all around the world came to compete.  There were even some Americans that came and tried to fight for the title.  It seemed like all the western teams got paired together to help eliminate them early, though it probably just seemed that way.  Like any celebration, this one had lots of food to eat.  Some traditional stuff to eat and some just really good food.  Kites lined the sky for the kite flying competition that happens the same day.


We came by a lake that once a year would hold an event where anyone willing enough was to swim across it.  It was in that same day that I saw how beautiful all of Taiwan really is.  The mountains are dramatic and abundant.  Each one is covered with trees and other vegetation.  It's an incredible sight to behold.


My experience learning the language thus far has been difficult.  It didn't realize how bad my language skills were until I found that I was grasping for words to form even the most simple of sentences.  But the most difficult part is learning the tones.  To help remind me how bad my tones are, I have the resident two year old making fun of me.
Speaking of which, just now we stopped off at a Gas Station to grab a few snacks for the road on our way east.  I tell you that Yuyin is a tricky girl.  We got some food that she isn't allowed to have cause it's spicy, something called pea crackers.   She kept asking for it and asking for it.  Finally, we got into the car and I was given them because I was in the front seat.  She asked me to give it to her, I of course told her I wouldn't give it to her.  She said, it's not for me, I'm going to give it to my mother.  She's a trickster she is.  I'm on to her game.


This has been written rather frantically, so it's probably pretty jumbled and terribly written.  All I can say is this, the past week has been exciting and confusing all at the same time.  I'm starting to understand the language more and more, but my speaking ability is just bad. I've seen and experienced more in one week then most do in one year.  That's probably an exaggeration, but I have seen a lot.  Likewise, I know I'll see more, but starting this week I have to hunker down and do what I came to do.  Hopefully my grasp of the language will increase over the next month or so.  We shall see.


@Tab

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: More posts, please!

item.title: Drivers In Taiwan
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
Driving in Taiwan is, I hate to stereotype, like many other East Asian countries is frightening. Taiwan has very little restriction on how people drive and more just offer suggestions that are never enforced. These simple restrictions are things like not running a red light... and well that's about it. The only other one that is enforced is speeding, but only because there are cameras setup around the entire country to catch people speeding. As a result you find that many people install sensors to just be able to slow down when one is approaching. There is one more restriction that people have, they need to wear a seatbelt in the front seat on the highway. That's right, they are not required to wear it off the highway and they're never required to wear it in the back seat and believe me no does wear them. Seldom do people even bother to wear them in the front seat even on the highway.
Now keep in mind, there really is any other restriction. What lane you're in doesn't really matter. Even if the lane is designed to be a turning lane, plenty people will just skip ahead and use that to cut in line. People will get into the oncoming lane whenever they feel like if they think it will get them past the person that is in front of them, not concerned with oncoming traffic of course. There is no law saying that you must yield to pedestrians, though you will get in trouble if you hit someone on the road. People will just park where ever they see fit. Plenty of times I've seen a person double parked on a busy road not in the car at all no where to be seen. This is illegal, but the police here just don't care or don't take the time to really enforce it. I saw one where the police officer gently tapped on the window and, more or less, encouraged the person to leave the spot they were doubled parked in. Mind you, no driver was present.
Terminology that is equivalent to cutting someone off or tailgating just doesn't exist here, because it's just how people drive. Scooters, which everyone here and I mean everyone here owns, weave in and out of traffic going from one lane to the next. Going between cars and even using the sidewalk, which by the way scared the crap out of me one time walking down the street. These scooters act just like cars to, people will haul just about anything on them. This includes four people at a time, dogs that rest where the feet go, ladders, and even, most frighteningly of all, giant propane tanks just hanging off the back.
The whole philosophy associated with driving is odd. Having only been able to experience a small amount including public transportation I can't speak for everyone. Bus drivers, unfortunately drive just like they would a car, breaking only when they absolutely have to, which means flinging me to the front of the bus at a bus stop. Car drivers drive exactly like the smaller scooter attempting to weave in and out of traffic, of course only as much as the car can fit between two objects. Scooters drive like they were walking through a crowd, using any small advantage they have to get in front and breaking like they can stop in half a foot.
All around it's a frightening experience, but it's not all bad. People who drive cars are rewarded every time they need to fill up on gasoline. First of all, every gas station is full service, but most amazingly you get a present at the end of your fill up. This gift usually amounts to a couple bottles of water or pack of tissue/napkins/toilet paper. I saw all three because usually those three things are wrapped into one package. Very seldom do you find soft tissue paper, or a roll of toilet paper, but more often a pack of napkins that is meant to use all of your cleansing needs.
@Tab

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Taiwan is crazy.

item.title: This past weekend
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
So far my trip here in Taiwan has been a lot of fun. There have been a few slow parts, but that's to be expected in a three month long journey. This past weekend was great and somewhat different from the rest of my time.
I was given the chance to just roam alone for a change, so I seized the opportunity. What I really wanted to do in that time, was just get a feel for the biggest city on the island, Taipei. I woke up, took a quick look at a few things I wanted to do around Taipei Main Station and head out. The train if I haven't said so before takes probably about 50 minutes to get from my home train station (Neili) to Taipei. It's a boring trip usually, I have to fight to get a seat and I get stared at by Taiwanese people. The past few times I've just been reading Moby Dick on my phone e-book app. When I finally arrived in Taipei I started my journey by checking out some local outdoor equipment stores, I learned pretty quickly that everything there wasn't any cheaper than at home, except for some killer deals on snow gear, surprisingly. Afterwards I set off to check out yet another Electronics paradise. Taipei is full of them, they have one that is actually a government owned computer megamart, it's amazing. Prices aren't anything spectacular, but I still need to do more research on the local companies that I might get the best deals on. That said, laptops from local companies tend to be more expensive here than in the US, imagine that. This place, by the way, was call the NOVA computer arcade.
After my brief shopping trip I set off on my cultural tour of the city, albeit not a very extensive tour. I hopped on the metro and went over to Chiang Kai Shek Memorial and after checking out the Theater and Concert Halls I finally saw him. Within the same hall a small show was going on where soldiers were showing off their gun twirling skills. I liked it, but they seem do this at every government owned facility. They did this at some hall near Taipei 101. About CKS, he was sitting there in much the same fashion as Lincoln at the Lincoln Memorial. Unfortunately, the two men seem to be nothing alike. They are considering removing CKS from the hall and renaming it because it is disputed the historical significance of this man.
After CKS I walked down the street to go to the 228 Memorial Museum and learn a bit about this historic event I know nothing about. First I went to lunch, I went to a place called Mos Burger, Japanese in origin if I remember correctly. To my surprise the only thing that they were out of on a burger menu was beef. I feel like it's only in East Asia I can go to a burger place and be told that all the beef is gone. After lunch I set off. On my way I came across something I didn't see on my map and to this day I'm not exactly positive what it is. A nice gentleman in broken english showed me the way to get inside. After going through a metal detector and getting some absolutely astonished looks from the people checking my bags, I found myself in front of a heavily western influenced mansion. As I said, I don't know what this place is, but I assume it was the presidential mansion that the Japanese constructed during the occupation of Taiwan. I went through the house, no English at all. The most I could do was go around and check look at the nice designs and wish I had a house like that. When I finally got to the garden, I came to a Japanese style tea house where a man in front wearing a hat that had in big letters "DORK" across the front greeted me. He explained what the tea house was about, unfortunately he did not say anything about the mansion, but at least I knew why the teahouse was there. I then set off for 228 memorial museum and after running around the memorial park for sometime l I finally came to the museum that was being renovated for some reason.
This was starting to be quite a nice walk. After all of the museum and memorial halls I decided to check out a few temples. The first one I was to go to was the Longshan temple. I suppose there isn't much to say about temples, past being temples, but they were beautiful nonetheless. Outside Longshan old men were playing a board game I was unfamiliar with and a slightly drunk man starting speaking chinese to me. Asking where I was from, and that's all I caught. A came to a few more temples and went to a small trade show, I think, where a very enthusiastic young man tried to get me to buy a chocolate covered banana. Three more temples later I went to a night market near the Longshan temple, where I may or may not have been asked if I wanted a prostitute, either that or I was asked to go and eat at a restaurant. It's hard to tell sometimes. If it was a prostitute it was cheap or at least I assume, if it was a restaurant it was expensive. She offered whatever service it might have been for about $650 NTD (approx. $20). After declining several times I got out and made my way to the Taipei 101.
I got to Taipei 101, and getting a bit nervous as I seem to do when things involve long elevator trips, I went down to the supermarket and bought myself half a liter of Taiwan Beer, sat down, and surfed the internet. I then took the trip, it happened to be about a dollar cheaper because I had a valid student ID. The fastest elevator in the world they claim, and sure enough, it was fast as hell. We climbed to the observation deck in some 39 seconds. In the process of slowing down I had some sensation of weightlessness too. It was crazy! Taipei 101 is gigantic, they have this crazy ball that hangs at the top that's supposed to prevent problems with wind. There is another one at the bottom to handle the earthquakes. The top gave me beautiful pictures of the surrounding area at night. Then came time to leave, and sure enough like any tourist area they tried to sell me stuff. The odd thing is it was clearly way out of my price range. It was all petrified coral that was over $1000 USD. I finally got down drank another liter of beer and set off for home. I got home and they tried to feed me even more!
Saturday done.
The next day Tony and Johnny came by to take me to a mountain further northeast. The most I can say is that we saw lots of wildlife and the pictures were pretty. We had a butterfly that just wouldn't stop bothering us. We kept getting lost and ended up taking the long way back. Tony not surprisingly was really sore the next day. We then went back into the nearby town, the name I do not remember, and got an assortment of mushrooms and some peaches. It was all very delicious. We checked out a nearby dam and the lake it created that supplies the water for the entire northern part of Taiwan. We then went into Dashi, walked across a huge bridge and picked up some dinner. I got to eat intestines, which were as you might expect, kinda chewy. Not very good either, but I was reassured that the food there was just not as good as it should be. Dashi is known for its Dougan, firm tofu, and we got some of that too, but that wasn't very good either. We then went to desert and that was subpar as well or at least I was being told that, it didn't seem to off to me at all. On our way back out we came to a man that was playing music on a leaf. I'm not joking, this man can play just about any song on a single leaf. The best part, is he taught us all how to do it. I could only make one bass note, but that's fine by me. How many people can play a leaf? We then came home, I was exhausted so I hit the bed quickly. And that for the most part was my weekend.
@Tab

item.title: ChungKing Mansions
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Sound ridiculously sketchy. Stay tuned for my post about my turkish adventure today, though. Sketchy, but still not quite as sketchy as this place you are describing. Stay safe. I hope you splurged and bought yourself a guidebook. There are few things more useful in a strange city. I love you and am looking forward to your pics!

item.title: Hong Kong
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
I had written a much longer and fully featured post about Hong Kong, but now that that is nearly a week away, I feel it'd be better to do a summary that hit on a few key points.
Hong Kong has several subtle things that make it feel like a European city with weird characters everywhere, but it is something else all its own. It's surprising that such a small area can have in one instance a busy metropolis, then going only forty minutes by train you can find a beautiful untouched landscape. I'm sure there are other places in the world like this, but, honestly, I can't think of any. The one thing that seems to have defined my stay there was the heat. Taiwan is hot, but Hong Kong, at least while I was there, was unbearable. I would walk a block and quite literally my shirt would be soaked and my nether regions were the swampiest of swamps.
During my stay I did a lot of touristy type things, like looking at temples. One temple, called the 10,000 Buddha temple, was just as it says, it had over 10,000 Buddhas. You can see the pictures online, it was quite the site. Other temple visits boasted the world's largest Buddha and wisdom trails.
I did my fair share of wandering around shops, looking at computer stores cause I'm a geek and going through street markets. I spent a whopping $17 USD during my stay on things I needed. The rest of my money went to sleep, food and the best part of all, drink. I don't drink that much in Taiwan, partially because the alcohol sucks, but mostly because I just work a lot and don't feel like going out to drink. Fortunately enough, I had a friend with me that gave me more reason to drink.
The few nights I went out I had some odd things happen to me. The first night I went to a pub and conversed with a Korean man and his son for a few hours only to finally come to the bill and he graciously paid for the whole thing, despite my objections. The next two nights I spent with my friend watching soccer from 2:30 AM til 5:00 AM. The first of those nights we spent it on Hong Kong Island which my hostel was not on and the subway stops running at 1:00AM. The next night we watched from outside a bar cause we didn't want to pay the $200 HKD($30USD). Every bar was doing it so there wasn't any point.
The next few days were a bit of a haze as I faded in and out of sleep. I still saw some cool stuff, went to the top of Victorian Peak on this unforgettably vertical decent up a mountain in a train.
In the midst of all this, I ate plenty of amazing food where most of it wasn't Kong Kong food, but amazing nonetheless.
Hong Kong is a crazy place. It's a lot of fun despite the heat, but I don't think I'd ever be able to go for more than a week.

item.title: Professional Programmer
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
It's come to my attention, as I look for a job in the states, that finding work as a programmer isn't going to be easy. Having never worked as a paid programmer, only IT with some programming, I can't attest to how much these companies actually want me to know. If it were as much as they they say then I would need to drop everything I'm doing to become intimately familiar with fifteen languages I've only dabbled with just for an entry-level position.
To put this in perspective, learning a new programming language can be equated pretty well to learning a spoken language. The concepts are easy to understand, the syntax can take awhile to grasp, but it can takes years to understand the subtleties. This is quite probably a gross exaggeration that many real programmers might disagree with, but it still provides some context.
If they don't expect that much from me and think my abroad work has some merit then I feel like I still don't come close to what is expected of me. Good thing I can interview well I suppose.
No lie, I saw a job ad that says it wants fresh or soon to be graduates with 1-3 years of professional experience in such-and-such language. I don't understand that at all. Maybe it isn't too far fetched to imagine that someone going through school has also been working full time as a programmer. I feel those people probably aren't going to be looking into entry-level work.
It's a crazy world out there, and it's gonna be fun trying to get a job in this terrible market. I think the best possible business venture I can have is developing android apps, like everyone else, and try to get some experience that way. The only problem with that though, is it goes back to what I said earlier. It's a pretty saturated market. I'm pretty sure it still doesn't have an auto-tune app, a free one for that matter.
Here's where it goes out to the people I know, all maybe three that read this blog. If you know anyone you could recommend me to, I won't stop you, hell, I'd even encourage it.
@Tab

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: I dig, though I believe you can get a job. You have the skills.

Mom is going to send you the contact info of the google guy. If you email me your final copy of cover letter and resume I will proofread it for you. You will find something, even if it isnt your dream job to start. Good luck!

I'm a programmer who isn't very good. I know a lot about how computers and people work and go from there. I'm not worried about you or your ability to grow to the situation, that's probably going to be your biggest selling point.

item.title: How the Taiwanese Work
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
Since I've only been here a little less than two months I am by no means an authority on the subject, but I thought I should talk about how Taiwanese work and what my typical day involves.
Let me start by covering what I do in a typical day. Every morning I wake up between 5:30AM - 6:30AM depending upon whether or not I exercise that morning. I walk upstairs into another bedroom, go out to an enclosed deck and turn on the hot water to take a shower. It's sort of an awkward experience, the shower head isn't suspended to the wall and the shower itself is surrounded by windows that looking out to tall townhouses that can without a doubt look in.
If I have enough time in the morning I run over to a local shop and buy two sandwiches for breakfast. Each one seems to be obligated to have cucumbers, egg and some sort of meat associated with it. I then sit on the couch waiting for Tony and Amily to come and pick me up and set out for work. Tony is crazy, he drives us all the way to work only to turn around and drive back into Taipei. The drive to work is at least an hour, often times about an hour and a half. It's a long drive, and it not exactly a normal commute to work here. I've said to people at work where I live here in Taiwan and they always respond the same way. "Oh! That is so very long away from here."
Then work is work. I am given small, usually ridiculous tasks, with no deadline and no expectation of completeness or quality. That's not important though. The people of Taiwan, at least where I've had the chance to experience it, take the term "the daily grind" to a whole new level. The time that you get into work isn't very important. People will filter in sometime around 9AM while others won't get until sometime after 10 o'clock.
Work is just as you might expect any other company in the States, some people work, while others will be talking or looking at something personal on their computer. This is until 12:01PM, lunchtime. Lunchtime is a big event, not in the respect that there's a lot going on, but more to do with how people treat lunchtime. From sometime around noon until almost 1:30PM the entire company is dead and the company makes an effort to make it that way. The lights are turned out and sleepy time music gets played over the intercom. Many people will eat really quickly and take long naps, while others, which is what I'm more familiar with, will grab lunch in large groups and eat together. The only thing that isn't done is work. It doesn't seem to matter how much work you have or what deadlines you need to keep, a Taiwanese lunch is used to unwind. Even if that means staying later, lunchtime and to a lesser extent dinner time is the no-work time.
After lunch it's back to the same thing as before, I continue my work and everyone else continues theirs. Some working until 6pm, but most will spend several extra hours working. For no rhyme or reason each of these people will work as late 9 - 10pm on a normal workday. This gets even worse if they have an approaching deadline. To this day I can't quite fathom why everyone works so late, they don't seem to be getting that much more work done.
When I finally leave, it's around 7pm, I walk over to the train station and if I have my climbing shoes with me, I'll go into the city and climb. That is another story all together.
When I finally get off the train where I live I take about a twenty minute walk home from the train station and walk in the door around 9pm if I do nothing else that day, but usually I get in around 11pm. Then Nainai makes sure I eat and after I eat, I go to bed.
@Tab

item.title: Bouldering in Taiwan
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
Before I came to Taiwan I looked for places to climb for while I was here. Unfortunately, any available literature was either more than ten years old or just didn't provide nearly enough information. Some would provide links to places that were only in Chinese or just wouldn't exist. So when I first came I used what little knowledge I had to find places to climb. The locals were even less help than the online guides. Most of the locals seem to believe that climbing wasn't something Taiwanese do.
After going over all of my options I finally looked up one from a guide about ten years old on the internet. It was intriguing, a climbing wall setup on the third story of an apartment building started by a climbing club that wanted a place to hang out and climb. At least to me this had sounded like an interesting group of people. The place was only marked by an unlit sign in Chinese at the base of the building. Don't ask me how I ended up actually finding it, but I did. My feeling of accomplishment was turned to uneasiness as I looked up a staircase that only slightly better lit than the sign. I had come this far, and refused to turn back. When getting to the top, I had been pleasantly surprised. A brightly lit climbing area with enthusiastic people doing crazy spiderman climbs all over the place.
The wall itself is very utilitarian and covers a space of about two medium sized living rooms. There are just walls, which encompass nearly the entirety of the space save a bathroom and a minuscule locker room in the back. There is also a tiny space to get chalk and read old Naruto Manga in Chinese. The climbing walls cover not only the walls, but take advantage of the entire ceiling as well. The crowd of people coming here encompass the entire "employee" roster which is somewhere in the range of about fifteen people. The rest of the people that come here are girlfriends of those climbers and random people including the expats. The wall is small and crowded to be sure, but the price and atmosphere are perfect. It costs a mere $50 NTD(approx. $1.50 USD).
It's pretty clear that this wall had been built to accommodate these guys love of climbing and to make a bit of money on the side. These guys are ridiculous, ripped out of their mind and doing some of the most elaborate climbing I've ever seen. I haven't seen this yet, but I've been told that these guys do something called "running ceiling laps." This is where they spiderman back and forth along the ceiling, I sight I can't wait to see.
In the two times that I've climbed there, I've tapered out after only about an hour with my arms feeling like jelly. This is mostly because I'm out of practice, but a lot is because of the unbelievable heat in this confined space that doesn't have air conditioning. Within only the first climb, sweat all ready starts to pour out of me.
Thankfully, like any climbing wall, it's a friendly bunch. There is a language barrier, but they all really nice and helpful people. The few expats their vary drastically, from being really friendly, to really quiet, to just being cold. It's an incredible place to say the least, and I look forward to use it as my training for Long Dong!
@Tab

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: It sounds like you work a crazy day, but I'm glad you have a chance to find some normalcy in climbing, even if it is a bizarre, hot place. I love you!
What an ambitious guy! I would have been spooked and turned around. I love you too!
Time for another post!

Ok, how did I not know you had a blog until Francis posted it on his reader...?!?
The climbing gym sounds fun. Rock on!

good old LZS! It's shutting down and moving to a new location in June actually!
I'm itching to go to Long Dong as soon as the weather clears up. If you're still in Taiwan, let's link up.

item.title: Angkor Wat Here I Come
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
There has been so much that has happened since my last post. I finished my work at Acer, drove across Taiwan, and flew to another island off of the island of Taiwan. I could write for a long time about each one of them, because each was unique and wonderful. That's not what I'm going to do.
I just came back from my trip to Penghu. It's that island off the coast of the island of Taiwan. Really it's an archipelago, but it's easier to refer to it as an island. It was relaxing trip. I learned how to surf from an Aussie that's been living on the island. In a crazy twist when visiting another still smaller island, I got pushed into a chinese only tour group and was ushered around in a bus all around the place. I'm sure the tour would've been a lot more interesting if I understood him completely, but I only caught a bit of it.
Now I'm sitting in an airport about to head to Siem Reap, the city that sits right next to Angkor Wat. It should be an interesting adventure to a recently de-landmined city that uses only US currency where I can ride elephants!
I don't actually have much to say, but I know I will be saying more as things come along.

item.title: Life without work
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: I think you are awesome and great and would make a valuable addition to any team. I can always look over your resume if you'd like another pair of eyes, or Jon because he has actually had to do it for work. I've heard it can take up to 1 month for every $10,000 you are looking to get paid, for whatever that is worth. Maybe you could try volunteering to make a website for a local charity to get more practice and get another thing on your resume?

item.title: Black Friday
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
Black Friday is that day where everything is so criminally discounted that people have been known to draw blood to take advantage of the savings. Last year people were even killed from being trampled on. Where the day before people have fallen into a tryptophan induced coma by four o'clock so they can wake up at the wee hours of the night to wait in line in an often very cold and sometimes, like today, very wet setting. To some people though it's well worth the savings.
Personally I don't understand Black Friday. I don't find it even remotely appealing to wait in line before the crack of dawn, even if it's for incredible savings. Granted right now I don't find it even remotely appealing to spend any sort of money. It's sort of hypocritical of me to say anything negative about those people that wait in line so early to get stuff on Black Friday. I've never been there for Black Friday, but one time I waited in line for a Wii. At least I was hanging out with people I knew, so it was fun, but still stupid.
What is good about Black Friday is that it signifies the official start of the Christmas Season. Though many stores have all ready started putting out Christmas merchandise as far back as September. Christmas is an exciting time of year, but it's importance to me is inline quite similarly with Thanksgiving. My favorite part about Christmas and Thanksgiving aren't about the presents or the food. It's always about seeing my family and being able to interact with them. All the other stuff are just perks.
Happy Thanksgiving to Everyone.
@Tab

item.title: Adding more space to an LVM2 Partition[repost]
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
So I'm not going to lie, I spent the better part of the morning trying to figure out how the hell I could resize my LVM2 Partition. I scoured the interwebs looking for answers, reading blog posts and what have you. To which I finally found enough information to get what I needed. I've decided to share this information with you. Let me establish that the scenario I will present to you will be just an example. Some variables and paths will need to be changed according to your own machine. This also assumes you have some extra partition that isn't currently being allocated. Also, you can read more about this from the following webpages:
  • http://www.howtoforge.com/logical-volume-manager-how-can-i-extend-a-volume-group
  • http://www.tcpdump.com/kb/os/linux/lvm-resizing-guide/expand.html
  • http://tldp.org/HOWTO/LVM-HOWTO/extendlv.html
DISCLAIMER: Perform this task at your own risk. Changing around partitions is dangerous and can prevent you from accessing your data and make it completely unrecoverable. If you do this, please don't for get to create BACKUPs. Our first task is to use that unallocated space and create another virtual partition. As either root or preferably as sudo, type: fdisk /dev/sda /dev/sda is interchangeable with whatever hard disk you have. You should see a screen come up with the prompt that looks like this. Command (m for help): This will be the command prompt we'll use to create a new partition. Type: Command (m for help): n l logical p physical p Assuming you want to use all of the unallocated space, just use the defaults. Now to change it over to a LVM, type: Command (m for help): t Partition number (1-3): 3 *This is the drive number that was created for me* Hex code (type L to list codes): 8e Command (m for help): w The partition has been allocated and now needs to have a few finishing touches for the next steps. pvcreate /dev/sda3 *Again, change depending on what you have.* Get the name of your Virtual Group that you'll be extending by typing vgdisplay With that name type the command: vgextend /dev/nameOfVG /dev/sda3 You've now extended the group, but now comes the part where you need to extend the actual LVM. Let's assume we want to extend our drive by 10 Gigs. Type the commands lvextend -L +10G /dev/NameOfVG/root With the drive extended we now have to fill it in with ext2/ext3 file formatting. Type the commands resize2fs -p /dev/NameOfVG/root It will inevitably complain about it being online, but will extend it regardless. If you are using a different type of file system other than ext2 then I suggest you refer to the third link. It gives detailed instructions on how to use the other file types. There you have now just extended your drive by 10gigs.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: What's up, just wanted to say, I loved this article. It was helpful. Keep on posting!

item.title: Learning the world
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to try an experiment. I'm going to use each successive month, until this time next year to learn something new. Something that is large enough that it will take the entire month to learn it. This includes things that are too large for the entire month, but not so large that by the end of it I've hardly scratched in a tiny bit of the surface. I'm looking primarily at creating a sort of working understanding of the subject by the end of the month. I hope to get a few things out of this. First and most obvious, I will have learned or have started my way on a new skill. Second and third are in my documenting of these adventures. In posting all that I know I will hopefully become more comfortable and better at writing and my documentation will hopefully help other around me. And finally, it will give me something interesting to do and I may even meet some new faces along the way. I need to set up some rules to go by:
  1. The topic I choose must be large enough to span an entire month.
  2. I must write about something every day, without fail
  3. If I miss one day then I have to write enough the next day to encompass that last one, if I miss that day then I need to write enough to encompass both and so on.
  4. Posting more than once a day will only marginally impact how much I am required to post the next day.
  5. I have to present something new every day in my post, and each post must be over 50 words.
  6. Days start at 6am. So if I post something at 3am on April 2nd, 2011 it counts for the day April 1st, 2011.
The previous rules may be broken in certain situations.  These include, but are not limited to situations that limit computer access and emergencies. I am allowed to add rules as I see fit. My first thing I will start with for the month of April is Ruby on Rails.  Tonight I will do a post on the things I will be working on.  This is a bit of a cheat because I have worked with rails before, but this will be a good start. Let the challenge begin!

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Good luck!

item.title: Ruby on Rails 3.0
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded: Oh, the evening. To start this challenge I need to create sort of a list of what to review and study and talk about, but because I've been terrible about, I'll start with a smaller list. Afterwards I will play the rest by ear. To have something to talk about tomorrow I'll start broad. Tomorrow, I'll discuss different development environments, the history and what it takes to create a rails application.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Writing is hard

item.title: Acts_as_indexed, things I've learned
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
Acts_as_indexed is a great utility for powerful, zero-dependency, full-text search on the Ruby on Rails framework.  Currently in its 0.7.2 release, this gem can be attached to any model to add search indexing to the data.  You can read more on the setup at https://github.com/dougal/acts_as_indexed. Here are a couple of things I couldn't find in the documentation. Resetting the index.   Say for any reason the index becomes corrupted or starts showing wrong data because the data in your database make a sudden change unexpectedly. You have to delete the /index directory that is automatically created. Just as simple as typing in your rails_root folder
rm -rf index/
This remove your index folder and when you run your app again this will reindex the whole thing and the indexing will be as good as new. This is pretty important when working in development as the data can change often.  You may want to consider turning this into a rake task as well. Version Control (Git, SVN, etc.) Make sure to ignore index/. For git in your rails_root folder you would type:
echo index/ >> .gitignore
This will append index/ to the end of .gitignore. There really isn't any reason to keep the index of your development data and your production or testing data in your repo. This isn't code and can vary widely from one setup to the next. I want to thank Douglas F. Shearer for putting together such a useful Gem. Again you can read more about this at https://github.com/dougal/acts_as_indexed. @Tab

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: I tried sphinx. It was a real pain in the ass, it was also overkill for what I was doing. Acts_as_indexed kept me from having to install other software to make it work. Sphinx is a lot more scalable, but I'm told Solr is even better for scaling, though it's supposed to be a lot slower initially.
It's funny you post this now, I spent all day working with sphinx a search indexer. <3

item.title: Rails 3: Helper within a layout
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
Someone may come along and tell me otherwise, but as of right now I have no idea how to pass a helper into a layout/template and yield the content in that layout.  I've tried a number of different ways of using the  <%= render %>, but it seems to be a fruitless endeavor. This may sound wildly confusing, so let me explain.  I've recently come across a problem at work where many of the things on our website are within a specific container, ie a box with a header over top and some special formatting for it.  So like any developer that likes to keep things DRY as possible, I thought, hell, why not just have some sort of default, make it a layout and just pass whatever html, whether it be partial or helper, to the layout. This way I don't have to keep on retyping everything. The idea was sound, and in practice it works when dealing with partials.  You can easily call the command in haml using
= render :partial => "/link/to/partial", :layout => "/my/sweet/layout"
And this works, but when you have html that being generated by a helper, it isn't possible to use any number of render setups (at least none that I've found) to pass that helper into the layout and output what you need. My short term solution, which may end up being my long term, is to setup a helper method within my main application that accepts html in the base and outputs the properly formatted html.  I hate this solution, because if I want to pass partials into this, I'm going to have to use the helper capture_html to my html to pass into the helper.  This seems like a lot of steps. Anyways, I'm off to research this a bit more. Hopefully I can find something a bit more meaningful. @Tab

item.title: China, over the half way point
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
To be fair, we're over the half way mark by about two days, but that doesn't mean I can't recap on what I've done.
It's been an interesting, yet brief trip. The primary reason for our visit, visiting my Dad, just flew by in the four short days that we had. And it seems like only yesterday that we showed up in Beijing and now we're leaving tomorrow. We have even less time in Taiwan, so I'm sure that will feel even more rushed.
Our first stop, Shanghai, was completely different, yet exactly the same as I remember it. Big, polluted and with to much emphasis on growth to feel like there is much history left. This was due part to the heavy amount of western culture that seems to dominate the city. The city itself is exploding, much like the rest of China, but all that seems to amount to right now is pollution that makes it hard to breathe and impossible to wear contacts without your eyes burning. That said, I still enjoyed Shanghai, mostly because I was able to visit my Dad, but also because of the foods and sights, but I really enjoyed how the city genuinely just felt alive and growing.
Shanghai gave us some interesting experiences as well, though it seems like I'm reliving many of the ones that I experienced while I was in China a few winters ago. Like before, we've been to the Chinese Venice, went to Shanghai Museum, and walked around the city feeling very out of place. One thing that we didn't try and I feel obligated to warn people about is the Shanghai Zoo. It reminds of a description I read of the Beijing zoo in years past, where it had a line that read something like, "The Chinese standards for taking care of animals are not on par with there western counterparts. If you have weak stomachs or children with you it is not recommended to go to this zoo." That's a very similar feeling I have with the Shanghai zoo. The cages are dingy, the animals are taken care of poorly, and this all surmounts to a truly distressing experience. Most notable is the pets exhibit, where they showcase caged pets such as dogs and cats, and other random ones like a porcupine. It was here that we saw every breed of dog you could think of, though we didn't actually attempt verify this. Each held in a cage not hardly shaded from the sun, concrete floors and smelled like burning dog feces. It reminded me a lot of the pound, but baking in the sun. The last straw of this trip was when we came to a corgi cage, I was speechless, and Becky burst into tears. I quickly grabbed her to make our way out, and we vowed never to return to the Shanghai zoo.
Now we're in Beijing. This is my city. I love it here. It seems like the proper mix of old and new. You'll have massive ten lane wide streets and then you'll go onto a small side road that you'd imagine were hundreds of years old. Yesterday we took a trip to the great wall, which no matter how great you imagine it being, still manages to surprise you with its immensity. It was a long hike, 7km to be exact, and although that may not seem to bad, that's going over hill after hill on less than intact steps and climbs in sweltering heat. There were a few times yesterday that Becky thought she was going to be sick with heat exhaustion. But we completed our trip, losing ten pounds in sweat and probably destroying our clothing in the process. Lets just say that the bus ride home had an interesting smell to it. Later that night we went back to where I studied while in Beijing. I showed a very tired, unenthusiastic Becky around the campus and found a place that sold DanBing! They are these pancakes that have an egg cracked in them with a sort of spicy sauce and some lettuce. They are about as simple as you can get, but boy are they delicious. We then found some random place to eat, we weren't hungry enough to eat the Duck we had planned to eat. Then we made our way home.
Today we head out for the summer palace and go around town a bit more. It should be fun, so long as I can get mor street food.
@Tab

item.title: My First Domain Name
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded: Though this sort of sounds out of place for a programmer, but I registered for my first domain name today. Well, technically not my first, I technically bought the domain animeday.com when I was in high school when I had this unending fascination with Anime. Today, however, marks my first. I registered my username tabfugnic.com and a bunch of other TLDs. I have no clue what I'm going to do with it, so currently they just reroute to my blog that I do a terrible job keeping up to date. Either way I'm sure I'll have fun with it, turning it into a project page or just a for funsies kinda web domain. Either way, just thought I'd share.

item.title: Rails 3 Routes: Namespace subdomains
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
I ran into an issue at work today.  We have our rails site and it is separated into four distinct areas, all doing different things, but all on the same IP address.  To make sure things don't collide, we namespace each section and reuse when necessary, a perfectly standard object oriented way to do things.  This creates a problem though. We wanted subdomains to correspond with the namespace instead of the typical rails way where the namespace would be found tacked on to the end of the URL like so, example.com/namespace/controller/action/id. There were examples all over the internet on how to do this, or at least part of this, but many involved complex situations using a plugins or programming your own middleware to handle all this. Most of what I found required that you write a lot of code and sometimes with random results. There were a few that gave real knowledge I was able to piece together what I learned from some of those sources and found a great solution, let me explain. Let's say for example that you have a blog application and you have put your blog stuff in a namespace called blog. Then let's say you have an admin section on this blog and you namespace that to be admin.  So you have links like example.com/blog/post/2 and to edit that you would go to example.com/admin/post/2 . Your routes.rb may look something like this: 
Rails.Application.routes.draw do
  namespace :blog do
    match 'post/:id' => 'post#show'
    match 'post' => 'post#index'
  end
  namespace :admin do
    match 'post/:id' => 'admin#show'
  end
end
Now lets say you've created the subdomains admin and blog to deal with these separately.  These subdomains undoubtedly point to the same place. So admin.example.com/blog/post/2 will point to the same place as blog.example.com/blog/post/2 despite having different subdomains. Using contraints we can now restrict the traffic and where it goes.  Contraints allow us to see the subdomain and match it on a regular expression, code would change to look something like this: 
Rails.Application.routes.draw do
  constraints(:subdomain => /blog/) do
    namespace :blog do
      match 'post/:id' => 'post#show'
      match 'post' => 'post#index'
    end
  end
  constraints(:subdomain => /admin/) do
    namespace :admin do
      match 'post/:id' =>'admin#show'
    end
  end
end
You'll notice that I've added contraints and within this contraint we have :subdomain which returns the name of the subdomain of our URL and the => /blog/ checks to see if the subdomain contains the word blog.  You can use any manner of regex in here and if it finds the expression then it will move on to the next item. So this is great, we now have links like admin.example.com/admin/post/2, but blog.example.com/admin/post/2 will not work, which is what we want.  But there is a small problem, admin.example.com/admin/post/2 is redundant. To remove this we can modify our code ever so slightly on the namespace line: 
namespace :blog, :path => '/' do
namespace :admin, :path => '/' do
These small little changes will mean that now things like blog.example.com/post/2 will be the same as the old example.com/blog/post/2, or admin.example.com/post/2 the same as the old example.com/admin/post/2 To me, this is as clean as you can get, but there is a drawback.  The changes put in place mean that we can no longer use our previous paths. They are gone, in favor of subdomains. I hope that helps someone down the line. @tab

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Noted and corrected. Thanks for pointing that out.
It seems your example code does not totally work, it has a syntax error where you use the :path variable. Check my gist https://gist.github.com/2357783 for the correct solution.
Wonderful! Exactly what I was looking for. (Kept hitting articles about how to handle wildcard subdomains instead of this context.)

item.title: Rails 3 Engines and Gems
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
This isn't going to be a really long posting on how to create and implement engines in your rails app. This is specifically regarding the gemspec and the gemfile in a rails engine. Within a rails engine you have a Gemfile which is used for your engine any time you need to use it as a standalone app. Then you have a gemspec which defines the parameters of your engine and the dependencies therein. Which means engines are really just treated as big gems that can be run as standalone apps, though I guess this isn't necessarily true.
Defining the parameters such as name, version and things like that are pretty straightforward, but the dependencies needs to be understood. Basically anything that is labeled as a dependency is exactly what it sounds like, it is dependent on that gem. If you are using bundler, then anytime you run a "bundle update" on the application that is using that engine will automatically install all dependencies. Which means you don't need to do any other leg work on the main application to get these files. Which also means that you don't need to explicitly declare these gems in your Gemfile in your main application, unless of course your main application needs it, that's just good environment setup.
The only reason I write about this is that there was some level of confusion amongst some of the people working on a project at work. We had developed three engines to plugin to our main app which more or less just acted as the intermediary between the three. Each of those engines was developed to be a standalone app. The thought had always been, whatever is declared in the gemspec must also be declared in the Gemfile in the main app. This turns out not to be true. Hope that helps.
One more fun fact. If you have a gemspec file, you can call that file in your Gemfile and it will maintain your Gemfile from your gemspec and not have to maintain two separate files.
@tab

item.title: Free up Mac Memory
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
For those of you who have always wondered how to free up a Mac's memory. For those just incredibly aggravated with consistent response from the Mac community, "Mac memory management is much better than PC," when asked this question. As if you asked, "How does a Mac and a PC compare when it comes to Memory management?" It's aggravating.
All you have to do is type in Terminal:
[code] purge [/code]

item.title: Raspberry Pi to arrive soon
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded: I just received an email from a company called element14.  The company is one of maybe two or three official distributors of the Raspberry Pi PC.  It's a micro computer about the size of a credit card, though considerably thicker. The purpose of the Raspberry Pi computer is to provide primary school students with an extremely cheap computer to learn how to program on.  It's also of course just a very cheap computer that can practically fit in your pocket.  Priced at $25 and $35 for the base RP and the RP with ethernet respectively, it's not hard to see how this is attractive.  Especially because the foundation claims that it is capable of supporting 1080p output out of the box. All ready people have started putting things like XBMC onto the thing.  Not only does it allow for software development of almost any kind, it also has additional headers to allow hardware hackers to have a hand at it.  It seems almost necessary as a toy for someone who just want s to endlessly tinker or even one who wants a low powered (only 3.5 watts to run it) computer or server. I'm pretty excited and so should everyone else, check it out at their website.  http://www.raspberrypi.org/

item.title: Keeping up with family
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
I've always been under the impression that keeping up with family is important. Calling them, not necessarily my forte, but I definitely let them know what I'm doing and will go out of my way, at times, to visit family. I don't see it as a chore, I genuinely enjoy visiting my family. They're always fun to be around, whether it be drinking or just chatting. The only issue is that my immediate family is no where near me. Most live in another state, some live in another country. That's just my immediate family.
When I say keep up with family, I mean all family. That includes the family I've somewhat been adopted into since I've been dating the same person for nearly six years. Today, I visited Becky's cousin's son's communion. Lots of ways that deviates from the family tree, but let me reiterate, Becky's Mother's Sister's Son's Son's first communion. I think the technical term is cousin once removed, I don't know. I've never been very good with how family tree structures work beyond cousin.
It was an all together pleasant occasion. People were friendly and the food was tasty. I myself drank a bit too much wine and ended up talking shop with some fellow software people that were beyond the scope of any relationship I'm willing to try to work out. The communion itself was fine. The priest had a long spiel about how breakfast, being the most important meal must be eaten everyday to stay strong. Likewise, since communion is important to maintaining good faith, it must be taken every week to keep up. It was long, convoluted, involved three trays of food and the priest with low self-esteem, needless to say, it was odd.
All of this effort was in part to keep up with family. Just to see how everyone was doing and let them know where I am. I'll do the same in about a month when I go to see my Grandparents in Alabama then again in about two months to see other side of the family. Every single one of them will be a different experience, but that doesn't mean that they will be any less enjoyable and everyone is just as important as the last.
@tab

item.title: Testing out Emacs blogging
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
So I'm going to be a teacher. Kind of crazy I think, but I'm immensely excited. Though as I am preparing, it's beginning to dawn on me more and more just how much of a responsibility I'm taking on. I'm going to be teaching for General Assembly's Back End Web Development class or BEWD for short. I have for the next nonconsecutive ten weeks or sixty hours a group of about twenty students without any prior knowledge of the software development. I will be teaching them how to use Ruby and Ruby-on-Rails to build functional web applications. Sixty measly hours is what I get with them, not nearly enough I think. but definitely enough to provide a very solid foundation.
I haven't actually had the chance to meet my students, though I don't imagine too many teachers get that chance before their first class. I keep being told in some way that I need to break it to them that developing software is hard. Yet despite not having even met them, I'm already confident that they are up for the task.
I look forward to seeing all of the various faces and background that come in to learn what software development is and whether or not it is something entirely for them. I also looking forward to using my own blog as a method to reflect on my tactics and the work I'm putting forward.
I'm sure whatever technique I end up moving forward with, I will get the chance to see

item.title: Teaching Ruby
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
So I'm going to be a teacher. Kind of crazy I think, but I'm immensely excited. Though as I am preparing, it's beginning to dawn on me more and more just how much of a responsibility I'm taking on. I'm going to be teaching for General Assembly's Back End Web Development class or BEWD for short. I have for the next nonconsecutive ten weeks or sixty hours a group of about twenty students without any prior knowledge of the software development. I will be teaching them how to use Ruby and Ruby-on-Rails to build functional web applications. Sixty measly hours is what I get with them, not nearly enough I think. but definitely enough to provide a very solid foundation.
I haven't actually had the chance to meet my students, though I don't imagine too many teachers get that chance before their first class. I keep being told in some way that I need to break it to them that developing software is hard. Yet despite not having even met them, I'm already confident that they are up for the task.
I look forward to seeing all of the various faces and background that come in to learn what software development is and whether or not it is something entirely for them. I also looking forward to using my own blog as a method to reflect on my tactics and the work I'm putting forward.
I'm sure however the class ultimately unfolds I will see what I hope to see. People who see it finally just click for them. That moment is going to be totally worth it.
@tab

item.title: And he starts with a wimper
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
Today was my day. It wasn't my first class, but it was my info session with my perspective students. Thus far, I haven't actually gotten a chance to really meet any of my students, but this past night was definitely the closest thing I had to it.
I'd say it went well, but that'd be a lie. I'd say I bombed completely, but that would also be a lie. I think I made the best of the evening, but, I need to preface anything that comes next, I was unsure what the whole thing all meant. I'll need to look through it, but according to the person organizing it, my understanding of the event was that it'd be incredibly informal. People would come in, I'd talk quickly about myself and my thoughts on the class and they'd ask a few questions.
Which is true, but I guess in my mind when I had heard it was informal, is that it'd be more meet and greet style. I'd get a chance to talk to each perspective student and really chat out what it is that they were looking for and what it was that I expected from them, but that wasn't exactly the case.
Since it's not officially the start of the class, I'm not going to fret about it too badly. It's certainly annoying because I hate feeling so unprepared. Come Tuesday, however, I'm going to absolutely destroy this class! Wooh!
@tab

item.title: First day of class
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
Subject: First day of class Keywords: Class, BEWD, ruby Date: 13 Nov 2013 00:16:06 -0500 From: tabfugnic@gmail.com Newsgroup: Tabfugnic Message-ID: --text follows this line-- I have to imagine everyone feels this way when attempting to teach a group of people. Everything goes far too fast. It's incredible because I have three hours to teach, you'd think there would be some excess.
This particular class was interesting though. This is deemed class zero. Rightfully so, because this particular class the students aren't meant to really learn anything, but they are supposed to have their environments setup and ready to go. Thankfully it appears I only have two windows users in my class, however one I need to look into because I don't know they're going to be able to work with Windows 8 and whether or not that's going to be a snag.
I tried something today that I guess has never been really tried before, but it seemed to go over very well. I tried a product inception for the whole class. Which wasn't perfect. I realize now that maybe twenty people were a bit too much. I think they genuinely saw it as insightful to see how others looked at the class and it provided an honest, if brief, forum for people to see what others were concerned about. In other words, it wasn't perfect, but it served its purpose.
The only other thing I can say about my class, is that I think I'll have a pretty good group. They all seem nice and eager. I have concerns already about one or two of them, but I am very happy with the people that have come. I'm really looking forward to when we finally get into the nitty gritty of the ruby language and later rails.
I get the feeling as well that this class will be a very humbling experience for me. I'm sure it will try my comfort in speaking, in my own knowledge of the language and, frankly, be incredibly draining. I'm much too excited for this class and look forward to Thursday already.
@tab

item.title: Real Class Begins
item.dc:creator: tabfugnic
item.content:encoded:
This week is supposed to be a more laid back week. That doesn't mean it feels like it. My class has begun and I still feel wildly under prepared, but am starting to feel better in my skin when standing in front of the class.
Class today started well enough with everyone feeling pretty comfortable in the Dev environment. The one user with windows has their Ubuntu USB key and for them it will be a bit of an upward battle learning how to use Ubuntu and keeping up with the rest of the team. The other one had a Windows 8 machine and after some serious troubleshooting, their environment was completely setup, so they fell a little behind.
There were some hitches here and there, github environments weren't properly setup, but all-in-all everyone saw the material and was really doing well and seemed to understand it. I'm very impressed with how well they're learning the material.
The biggest fall was timing. Here I am, concerned that I was going to have an hour left at the end of class, but instead I cut so close and ultimately never got a chance with helping them setup a blog.
There are clearly places I feel I need to improve. I think I need to consolidate my information and not worry about running under. Which leads me to preparing for downtime when I present. I think it will take some time to really feel comfortable with the timing, but I'm definitely getting there.
I need to provide slides for people to go along with. I think it'd be good to actual be at the keyboard more. Get people coding more. I would like to become more inventive with the exercises. Finally, I want my passion for my job to be crystal clear, though I think that will come soon.
I'm very happy with how things are progressing and think that they will only start getting better.
@tab
